exemplar.title: Investigating the Impact of Distraction on Recency Effects

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Impact of Distraction on Recency Effects

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim and relevance are clearly stated and meaningfully linked to student study strategies.
MultiâStore Model and serialâposition effects are accurately described and connected to the investigation.
Hypotheses are explicit, with IV and DV correctly identified and fully operationalized.
weaknesses: Relevance explanation, while clear, lacks the full sophistication expected for the top band.
Citation of McLeod lacks a publication date, reducing theoretical credibility.
Could further detail sample characteristics when describing Glanzer & Cunitz to strengthen context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Based on strand assessments (5, 5, 6), the student meets band 5 overall. The aim is clearly explained and relevant, the theoretical background is accurate and linked, and hypotheses are fully operationalized. Minor brevity in explaining educational relevance and incomplete citation details prevent a topâband 6.

strengths: Choice of independentâmeasures design is well justified, showing understanding of order effects and trade-offs.
Convenience sampling is clearly described and its limitations (bias, generalizability) are explained.
Controlled variables and materials are thoroughly defined and each control is justified for validity.
weaknesses: Procedural description is cut off in places, affecting replicability.
Random assignment procedures are not fully detailed, weakening internal validity discussion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With strand assessments all at band 4, the exploration section demonstrates clear explanations of design, sampling, and controls. Minor gaps in procedural detail and randomization prevent perfection but justify the full band-4 score.

strengths: Descriptive and inferential statistics (mean, SD, t-test) are appropriately applied and linked to the hypothesis.
Graphs (bar chart with error bars and box plot) are correctly presented and explicitly address the hypothesis.
Findings are interpreted in the context of the data and connected back to theory, acknowledging insignificance.
weaknesses: Inconsistency in reported p-values across text undermines reporting accuracy.
Descriptive table omits mode and median entries, making analysis incomplete.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 5, 5, and 5 indicate strong, accurate analysis. Statistics are applied correctly, graphical presentation is appropriate, and interpretations link to the theory. Minor formatting and consistency errors prevent a perfect band-6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to the Multi-Store Model and Glanzer & Cunitz.
Strengths and limitations are relevant, explained, and tied to validity of the investigation.
Modifications are explicitly linked to stated limitations and justified coherently.
weaknesses: Discussion is somewhat surface-level, lacking consideration of effect sizes.
Claim of high reliability lacks empirical backing (e.g., inter-rater checks or replication data).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Given strand assessments (4, 5, 5), the evaluation shows sound discussion of theory, thorough strengths/limitations analysis, and justified improvements. Some depth (effect size discussion, reliability evidence) is missing, so band-5 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with error bars and a box-and-whisker plot are both suitable for the data and explicitly address the hypothesis.  Axes are labelled and values shown.  Small aesthetic flaws (slightly blurred exported images) keep this at 5 rather than 6.

Findings are interpreted against the numerical outcomes (mean difference 0.51, p = 0.147) and linked back to accepting the null.  The interpretation is tied to the theory (STM rehearsal) and acknowledges insignificance.  Depth is good but not exceptionally nuanced; thus 5.

Both null and research hypotheses are written; IV (presence/absence of 30-s distraction) and DV (number of last-five objects recalled) are correctly identified and fully operationalised.  No ambiguity is present, matching the top of the band, so 6 is awarded.

Convenience sampling is described and justified (timetable access), with limitations (bias, generalisability) clearly explained.  Participant characteristics (age, English fluency, developmental stage) are articulated.  This meets the explanatory level, meriting the top of the band (4).

The Multi-Store Model, serial-position effects and Glanzer & Cunitz are accurately described, and each component is explicitly connected to the present investigation (distraction should block rehearsal/STM).  The explanation is accurate but not exhaustively detailed on mechanisms such as decay vs. displacement, hence band 5 rather than 6.

The student relates results to Glanzer & Cunitz and the Multi-Store Model, discussing why their findings diverge.  While coherent, the discussion is somewhat surface-level (e.g., no detailed consideration of effect sizes), fitting band 3-4 and earning 4.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate whether a 30-s distraction removes the recency effect â and then explains why this is educationally relevant (studentsâ study strategies).  The relevance is explicit and goes beyond a mere mention.  Minor brevity in the classroom relevance prevents the full sophistication expected for 6, so 5 is most fitting.

Multiple strengths (independent design, standardisation) and limitations (small sample, prior exposure, English proficiency) are each explained for their impact on validity.  Clear relevance to hypothesis is maintained.  Depth is strong but a few points lack empirical support, so 5.

Controlled variables are identified and each is linked to why control is needed (lighting/noise, 5-s display, standardised counting).  Materials list is complete and justified (objects chosen for familiarity and STM overload).  These explanations satisfy the upper band.

Proposed improvements (larger sample, stricter classroom control, object size standardisation, use of words) are explicitly tied to stated limitations and justified.  The rationale is coherent though not exhaustive, matching the criteria for 5 within the top band.

The student not only describes but also explains why an independent-measures design was chosen (avoiding order effects/demand characteristics) and recognises trade-offs (participant variability).  This aligns with the âexplainedâ descriptor; minor omissions on counterbalancing nuances keep it at 4 rather than perfect.

Appropriate descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an independent-samples t-test are applied correctly; normality was checked.  Calculations and decision rule (p > 0.05; fail to reject H0) are accurate.  Minor formatting issues in the table/SD labelling prevent a perfect 6 but overall accuracy is high, warranting 5.

annotations.comment: The title is concise and descriptive, clearly indicating the topic and referencing the key study by Glanzer & Cunitz.
The citation of Mcleod lacks a publication date, reducing the credibility of the theoretical background. Include full date information.
Effectively links the serial position effect to the Multi-Store Model, explaining primacy and recency with clear terminology.
The description of Glanzer & Cunitzâs procedure is accurate but could be strengthened by specifying sample characteristics and task timing to mirror the original.
The aim and relevance are clearly articulated, linking distraction tasks to student study strategies in a meaningful way.
Hypotheses are explicit and operationalized, correctly identifying the IV as the 30 s distraction and the DV as number recalled.
The choice of an independent-measures design is well justified to avoid order effects, demonstrating an understanding of design trade-offs.
The design section would benefit from clarification of random assignment procedures to strengthen internal validity.
Convenience sampling is described with justification and limitations, linking accessibility to potential bias.
Consider discussing demographic factors (e.g., gender balance) that might further impact generalizability.
Participant age, cognitive stage, and language fluency are well detailed, supporting suitability of the sample.
Independent, dependent, and several controls are clearly defined and operationalized, demonstrating thorough variable management.
Table 1 lacks entries for mode and median under headings, making descriptive analysis incomplete and unclear.
The explanation of handling spelling variations (e.g., âcombâ vs. âbrushâ) demonstrates transparent data cleaning practices.
The inclusion of both bar chart and box plot is appropriate and directly addresses the hypothesis regarding group differences.
Interpretation links mean difference to the alternate hypothesis, showing a clear connection between statistics and theory.
The discussion of overlapping error bars and their implications for significance demonstrates a nuanced understanding of variability.
Using range alongside SD enriches the variability analysis and supports interpretation of group consistency.
Normality testing is explained clearly and justifies the use of an independent-samples t-test.
The reported P-value (p=0.0736) contradicts elsewhere (p=0.1472); ensure consistency in inferential statistics reporting.
Expand the evaluation of dual-task interference by discussing how tasks may differentially engage phonological vs. visuospatial subsystems.
Clarify how the control of STM decay was measuredâspecify the decay threshold or rationale behind 45 s total display time.
Materials list is complete; to strengthen justification, briefly explain why each item is essential for standardization.
The procedural description is incomplete due to a cut-off sentence; ensure the full procedure is detailed for replicability.
Ethical considerations are comprehensively addressed, covering consent, withdrawal rights, confidentiality, and debriefing.
There is a minor formatting inconsistency: the SD values are misaligned from their labels. Ensure table formatting matches text discussion.
The evaluation acknowledges statistical insignificance and challenges to reliability, relating findings back to theoretical expectations.
Identifies participant variability and timing factors effectively, demonstrating reflection on internal validity threats.
Proposed sample size increase and stratified sampling are explicitly tied to stated limitations, showing justified improvements.
The conclusionâs claim of high reliability lacks empirical support such as inter-rater checks or replication details.
Consider calculating and discussing effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) to add depth to the statistical evaluation.
Works Cited entries show inconsistent formatting styles. Adopt a single citation style throughout (e.g., APA or MLA).
